ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for tau sar piah. A0CKP (Biscuits, cookies and similar small baked products) is the closest defensible parent category for this flaky baked pastry.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of sweet tau sar piah weighs approximately 60 g, which is a typical single-serve portion as sold at Singaporean bakeries and coffeeshops.

nutrient_source.title: Tau Sar Piah (Sweet Mung Bean Pastry) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA SR Legacy analogous estimation for Chinese-style filled pastry
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists tau sar piah as a traditional Singaporean snack. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo data for tau sar piah (sweet variety) cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entries for mung bean paste pastry components. Per-100g values are derived from a typical formulation: flaky wheat-flour pastry shell (~40%) filled with sweetened mung bean paste (~60%), baked with lard or shortening. Energy and macros are consistent with HPB Foodo reported values for similar filled pastry items (approximately 400–430 kcal/100g range).

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mung bean-based products and analogous filled pastry — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tau sar piah in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 55 (low) based on: (1) mung bean paste has a reported GI of approximately 31–39 (low), but the pastry shell is made from refined wheat flour with added sugar and fat, which elevates the composite GI; (2) analogous Chinese filled pastries with sweetened bean paste and wheat flour shells are reported in the range of 50–60 in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); (3) the fat content from lard/shortening moderates the GI relative to plain wheat products. A composite GI of 55 is a defensible central estimate. Glycemic load per serving = (55 × 34.5g available carbohydrate per 60g serving) / 100 = 19.0, rounded to 19.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~180 mg/100g; ~108 mg per piece) and saturated fat from lard
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit tau sar piah to occasional consumption, as the sodium from the pastry and the saturated fat from lard contribute to cardiovascular load; one piece provides approximately 108 mg sodium, which is manageable within a 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target if other meals are low-sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~55) and moderate glycemic load (~19 per piece) driven by refined wheat flour pastry shell and sweetened mung bean filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat tau sar piah as an occasional snack rather than a regular item; the low GI of approximately 55 and a glycemic load of ~19 per 60g piece can cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise, and portion control to half a piece with a low-GI meal is advisable to limit glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~7.2g/100g; ~4.3g per piece) from lard or shortening used in the flaky pastry layers
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit tau sar piah due to its saturated fat content from lard-based pastry, which can raise LDL cholesterol; one piece provides approximately 4.3g saturated fat, representing over 20% of the recommended daily limit of 20g, and should be consumed infrequently as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet Tau Sar Piah (甜豆沙饼) is a traditional Singaporean and Teochew baked pastry consisting of a multi-layered, flaky wheat-flour shell encasing a smooth, fragrant sweetened mung bean (green bean) paste filling. The pastry shell is made by laminating a water-dough outer layer with a lard-enriched inner dough, creating the characteristic flaky, crumbly texture that distinguishes tau sar piah from other Asian pastries. The mung bean filling is prepared by cooking split mung beans until soft, then blending with sugar and sometimes a small amount of oil to produce a smooth, mildly sweet, and subtly earthy paste. The assembled pastry is baked until golden. Each piece typically weighs 55–65g and is sold individually at traditional Singaporean bakeries, coffeeshops, and hawker stalls. Nutritionally, one piece provides approximately 251 kcal, 6.8g protein, 11.1g fat (including ~4.3g saturated fat from lard), and 34.5g carbohydrate with 1.7g dietary fibre. The low glycemic index (~55) and glycemic load (~19 per piece) mean it causes a moderate postprandial glucose rise. The saturated fat from lard is the primary cardiovascular concern. Tau sar piah is a culturally significant snack in Singapore's Teochew and Hokkien communities, commonly consumed as a morning or afternoon snack with kopi (local coffee) or tea. It is not suitable for halal diets due to lard content unless specifically certified halal with vegetable shortening substitution.

confidence_reason: HPB Foodo lists tau sar piah as a recognised Singaporean food item. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo data for similar filled pastry items and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy component data for mung bean paste and wheat-flour pastry. No single exact per-100g entry for sweet tau sar piah was available with full micronutrient breakdown; therefore confidence is medium rather than high. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature on similar filled pastries.
notes: 1. Serving size of 60g is based on typical commercial tau sar piah weight as sold at Singaporean bakeries including Toast Box. 2. Lard is the traditional fat used in the flaky pastry shell; some commercial producers substitute vegetable shortening for halal certification — this record reflects the traditional lard-based formulation. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted HPB Foodo / USDA source data for this pastry category. 4. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source data. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source data. 6. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source data. 7. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source data. 8. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source data. 9. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source data. 10. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as mung bean paste pastry contains no significant vitamin C; the baking process destroys any residual trace amounts, confirmed consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for similar baked bean paste products. 11. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for tau sar piah is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 12. The cuisine tag 'Toast' in the input reflects the field survey restaurant category (Toast Box) rather than the food's actual cuisine classification; this record correctly classifies the food as Singaporean/Teochew. 13. Aliases consistency verified: every string in aliases_by_language (english, chinese, malay, tamil, hindi, romanized) appears verbatim in the flat aliases list. The previous iteration contained a mixed-script string in the flat aliases list that did not match the pure Devanagari Hindi entries in aliases_by_language; this has been corrected by ensuring all flat alias strings are copied exactly from aliases_by_language values.
